summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to Ethan Zuckerman's Atlantic piece about advertising as the internet's original sin. While she agrees with his core argument about the inherent conflict between privacy and ad-based business models, she pushes back on his claim that users have simply 'accepted' manipulation because they haven't left Facebook. She argues that addiction and habit explain continued use better than acceptance, drawing an analogy to smoking. The post concludes by asking what social networking alternatives might emerge and whether privacy will become a luxury only premium users can afford.
key_points: The ad-based internet economy creates an inherent conflict with privacy, requiring ever-deeper surveillance to improve targeting
Continued use of manipulative platforms like Facebook reflects addiction and habit, not acceptance of manipulation
User sentiment toward Facebook has shifted even if behavior hasn't yet caught up
The real question is what alternatives people will eventually leave Facebook for
Privacy may become commoditized, with protection available only to those who can pay
money_quotes: It is a mistake to assume that 'users accept this sort of manipulation' because not everyone has stopped using Facebook.
People don't give up addictive habits overnight. But it doesn't mean they are feeling the same way about Facebook that they did 4 years ago.
People are adjusting their opinion of the user experience as that user experience is increasingly manipulated and creepy.
An analogy should be drawn to something like smoking, where the rates have gone way down since we all found out it is bad for you.
What is the social networking model of the future?
Will privacy be entirely commoditized, whereby only premium pay members will be protected, while the rest of us will be thrown to the dogs?
key_insight: Confusing users' continued engagement with manipulative platforms for acceptance ignores the stickiness of addiction—the real question is what they'll eventually leave for.
